title: Applying wood wall cladding
subtitle: Fix tongue-and-groove or shiplap boards to an interior wall
excerpt: How to fix tongue-and-groove or shiplap timber cladding to a wall: setting out battens, fixing the first board level, secret-nailing subsequent boards, and finishing around sockets and switches.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: A horizontal or vertical strip of timber fixed to a wall to provide a fixing point for cladding, board, or other surface material. In wood cladding, battens run horizontally (for vertical boards) or vertically (for horizontal boards), spaced at approximately 400 to 600 mm centres.

A method of fixing tongue-and-groove boards where the nail is driven at an angle through the tongue of each board so it is hidden by the groove of the next board placed. The nail head is invisible in the finished surface.

content.text: Wood cladding on an interior wall covers damaged plaster, adds thermal mass, and brings character to a featureless room. Tongue-and-groove boards are the standard domestic finish; shiplap (overlapping rebated boards) is more typically found in utility rooms and outbuildings. Either type is fixed to
that are plugged and screwed to the wall first. The cladding boards are then
through the tongue so no nail heads show in the finished face.
Sweep a cable-and-pipe detector across the wall before fixing any batten or drilling any plug hole. Cables run vertically from sockets and horizontally from switches; mark any hits before drilling.

Tongue-and-groove boards (allow 10% extra for waste and matching), 50 x 25 mm treated softwood batten, No. 8 x 50 mm screws and wall plugs, 40 mm lost-head nails or 40 mm brad nails for the pin hammer, a spirit level, a tape measure, a crosscut saw, and a carpenter's pencil. Acclimatise the boards in the room for 48 hours before fixing; timber moves with humidity.

Mark horizontal lines across the wall at 400 mm centres using the spirit level. Fix a batten on each line, drilling and plugging into the masonry or screwing into studs. Check each batten is level and sits flat to the wall; pack behind any batten that rocks with slim timber shims. Fix a batten along the top and bottom of the wall as well.

Start in a corner with a board that has its groove toward the wall. Check it is plumb with the spirit level; this first board sets the alignment for every subsequent board. Fix it through the face with a nail into each batten, punching the nail head below the surface; this board's face nail will be hidden by the corner trim later.

Engage the tongue of the second board into the groove of the first. Tap it tight with a block of scrap wood to avoid bruising the tongue. Drive a lost-head nail at 45 degrees through the tongue at each batten position. The nail head should be set below the tongue face so the next board's groove slides cleanly over it. Continue across the wall, tapping each board tight before nailing.

At internal corners, the boards from each wall simply butt together; the joint is covered by a length of quadrant moulding pinned into the corner. At external corners, cut the boards at 45 degrees on the mitre saw and glue and pin the mitred boards together. At skirting board level, remove the skirting before fitting the cladding, then refix the skirting over the front face of the cladding.

attrs.symptom: Boards cupping and pulling away from battens
attrs.cause: Boards were not acclimatised before fixing and have responded to the room's humidity after installation.
attrs.fix: Remove the boards, stack them flat in the room for 7 days before refixing. Consider a vapour barrier behind the battens in particularly humid rooms.

attrs.symptom: Boards running out of plumb across the wall
attrs.cause: First board was not set perfectly plumb, and the error compounded with each subsequent board.
attrs.fix: Every few boards, check the board face with the spirit level and tap it plumb before nailing if any drift has occurred.
